text: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.
Plants use photosynthesis to convert light energy, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose and oxygen.
Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells from one parent cell and is used for growth and repair.
DNA is a double-helix molecule composed of nucleotide base pairs: adenine pairs with thymine, and guanine pairs with cytosine.
Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells from one diploid parent cell through two rounds of division.
Cellular respiration converts glucose and oxygen into ATP, carbon dioxide, and water to release usable energy for cells.
Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions without being consumed in the process.
ATP (adenosine triphosphate) stores and transfers chemical energy within cells; energy is released by hydrolysis of its phosphate bonds.
Osmosis is the net movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane from lower to higher solute concentration.
Diffusion is the net movement of molecules from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration until equilibrium is reached.
The Krebs cycle occurs in the mitochondrial matrix and generates NADH, FADH2, and CO2 from acetyl-CoA per turn.
Natural selection acts on phenotypic variation, favouring traits that improve survival and reproduction in a given environment.
Energy flows through an ecosystem from producers to primary, secondary, and tertiary consumers along a food chain.
Mendel's law of segregation states that allele pairs separate during gamete formation and recombine randomly at fertilisation.
During translation, ribosomes read mRNA codons and join amino acids in sequence to build a polypeptide chain.
The nucleus contains the cell's DNA and controls gene expression and cell reproduction.
Mitochondria produce most of the cell's ATP through oxidative phosphorylation and are bounded by a double membrane.
Bacteria are prokaryotes: they lack a membrane-bound nucleus and replicate by binary fission.
Viruses must hijack a host cell's machinery to replicate because they cannot reproduce independently.
A point mutation changes a single nucleotide in the DNA sequence and may alter the resulting protein or have no effect.
An ecosystem includes all living organisms in an area as well as the abiotic factors with which they interact.
Homeostasis is the process by which an organism maintains stable internal conditions such as temperature, pH, and blood glucose levels.
Plant cells differ from animal cells by possessing a rigid cell wall, a large central vacuole, and chloroplasts.
Gene expression involves transcription of DNA into mRNA in the nucleus, followed by translation of mRNA into protein in the cytoplasm.
Biodiversity refers to the variety of species, genetic variation within species, and diversity of ecosystems in a given region.
A dominant allele masks the effect of a recessive allele when both are present in a heterozygous genotype.
The lymphatic system transports lymph fluid, absorbs dietary fats from the gut, and supports immune defence throughout the body.
The rough endoplasmic reticulum is studded with ribosomes and is the primary site of protein synthesis for secretion.
Humans have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 homologous pairs; the X and Y chromosomes determine biological sex.
Transpiration is the evaporation of water from plant leaves, creating a pressure gradient that draws water up from roots.
The scientific classification hierarchy from broadest to most specific is: Domain, Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species.
Active transport moves molecules against their concentration gradient and requires energy in the form of ATP.
Epigenetics studies heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence.
Chloroplasts contain the green pigment chlorophyll, which absorbs light energy used to drive photosynthesis in plant cells.
Mutualism is a symbiotic relationship in which both species benefit, such as the relationship between bees and flowering plants.
In codominance, both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote, as seen in the AB blood group where both A and B antigens are present.
Population ecology studies the size, density, distribution, and dynamics of groups of individuals of the same species in an area.
Competitive inhibitors bind the active site of an enzyme and reduce reaction rate; their effect can be reversed by adding more substrate.
Apoptosis is programmed cell death, a tightly regulated process that removes damaged or unnecessary cells without causing inflammation.
The nervous system comprises the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and the peripheral nervous system (all other nerves).
The periodic table organises elements by increasing atomic number and groups elements with similar chemical properties together.
An atom consists of a positively charged nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by negatively charged electrons.
Ionic bonds form when one atom transfers electrons to another, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other electrostatically.
Covalent bonds form when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons to achieve a stable full outer electron shell.
Matter exists as solid, liquid, gas, or plasma; the state depends on temperature and pressure relative to a substance's physical constants.
In an exothermic reaction, energy is released to the surroundings; in an endothermic reaction, energy is absorbed from the surroundings.
Le Chatelier's principle states that a system at equilibrium will shift to minimise the effect of any imposed change in conditions.
An acid donates protons (H⁺) to a solution and has pH below 7; a base accepts protons and has pH above 7; pH 7 is neutral.
Oxidation involves the loss of electrons; reduction involves the gain of electrons; both always occur together in a redox reaction.
Hess's law states that the total enthalpy change of a reaction is the same regardless of the pathway taken from reactants to products.
A solution is a homogeneous mixture in which a solute is uniformly dissolved throughout a solvent.
Boyle's law states that at constant temperature, the pressure and volume of a fixed amount of gas are inversely proportional.
One mole of any substance contains approximately 6.022 × 10²³ particles, a value known as Avogadro's number.
Molarity expresses solution concentration as the number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of solution.
Metals are generally good conductors of heat and electricity, malleable, ductile, and have a shiny lustre.
Organic chemistry studies carbon-containing compounds; carbon's four covalent bonds enable enormous structural and molecular diversity.
Increasing temperature raises reaction rate because a greater proportion of molecules exceed the activation energy per unit time.
Electrolysis uses electrical energy to drive non-spontaneous chemical reactions, commonly used to extract or purify metals.
Group 1 elements (alkali metals) react vigorously with water to produce a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas.
Electrons occupy atomic orbitals described by four quantum numbers: principal (n), angular momentum (l), magnetic (mₗ), and spin (mₛ).
A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy and is not consumed in the reaction.
Isomers are compounds sharing the same molecular formula but differing in the structural arrangement of their atoms.
A buffer resists pH changes when small amounts of acid or base are added, owing to its weak acid and conjugate base content.
Complete combustion of a hydrocarbon in excess oxygen produces only carbon dioxide and water as products.
Polymers are large molecules formed by covalently linking many repeating monomer units, as seen in plastics, rubber, and proteins.
Electronegativity is an atom's tendency to attract shared electrons; large differences between bonded atoms produce polar or ionic bonds.
Across a period in the periodic table, atomic radius generally decreases as nuclear charge increases while electrons fill the same shell.
Titration determines the concentration of an unknown solution by reacting it with a standard solution of known concentration to a visible endpoint.
Nucleophilic substitution occurs when a nucleophile replaces a leaving group on a carbon atom, as commonly seen in halogenoalkane reactions.
A reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous when the Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) is negative under the given conditions.
Functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH), carbonyl (C=O), and carboxyl (–COOH) determine the chemical properties of organic molecules.
A mixture can be separated by physical methods such as filtration, distillation, chromatography, or evaporation.
A reaction is spontaneous when Gibbs free energy change (ΔG = ΔH − TΔS) is negative; at equilibrium ΔG equals zero.
The empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound, while the molecular formula gives actual atom counts.
Solubility is the maximum mass of solute that dissolves in a fixed volume of solvent at a given temperature; it varies with temperature.
Newton's first law states that an object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force.
The law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
A wave transfers energy without transferring matter and is characterised by its amplitude, wavelength, frequency, and speed.
Gravity is an attractive force between any two masses; its strength decreases with the square of the distance between the objects.
Ohm's law states that the current through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage and inversely proportional to the resistance.
Einstein's special theory of relativity holds that the speed of light in a vacuum is constant for all inertial observers.
The second law of thermodynamics states that the total entropy of an isolated system always increases over time.
Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave requiring a medium to propagate; it cannot travel through a vacuum.
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle states that the more precisely a particle's position is known, the less precisely its momentum can be determined.
When light passes from one medium to another it changes speed and bends; this bending is called refraction and is described by Snell's law.
Speed is the scalar measure of how fast an object moves, calculated as distance divided by time.
The law of conservation of momentum states that the total momentum of a closed system is constant when no net external force acts on it.
Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller nuclei, releasing energy due to the conversion of a small amount of mass.
A magnetic field is produced by moving electric charges and by the intrinsic magnetic moments of certain elementary particles.
The electromagnetic spectrum ranges from low-energy radio waves to high-energy gamma rays; all travel at the speed of light in a vacuum.
Half-life is the time required for half the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay; it is constant and characteristic of each isotope.
Pascal's principle states that pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to all parts of the fluid.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time and is a vector quantity with both magnitude and direction.
The photoelectric effect shows that light ejects electrons from a metal only if each photon has sufficient energy, confirming light's particle nature.
Work is done when a force causes displacement along the direction of the force; power is the rate at which work is done over time.
In a series circuit, the same current flows through all components; in a parallel circuit, the voltage is equal across each branch.
Nuclear fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier nucleus, releasing energy; it is the process that powers the Sun and other stars.
Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid displaced by that object.
A convex (converging) lens bends parallel light rays to a focal point; a concave (diverging) lens spreads them apart.
Specific heat capacity is the energy needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C and determines how quickly it heats or cools.
Coulomb's law states that the electrostatic force between two charges is proportional to their product and inversely proportional to the square of their separation.
Newton's third law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction; forces always occur in pairs.
In simple harmonic motion, the restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium, producing sinusoidal oscillation.
The Doppler effect is the observed change in wave frequency when the source and observer move relative to each other.
Gravitational potential energy equals mass × gravitational field strength × height above a reference point (E = mgh).
Total internal reflection occurs when light in a denser medium strikes the boundary at an angle exceeding the critical angle and reflects entirely back.
Scalars have magnitude only (e.g. speed, temperature); vectors have both magnitude and direction (e.g. velocity, force).
Wave-particle duality states that all matter and energy exhibit properties of both waves and particles depending on how they are measured.
A capacitor stores electrical energy in an electric field between two conductive plates separated by an insulating material.
Electrical power is the rate of energy transfer in a circuit, calculated as voltage multiplied by current (P = VI), measured in watts.
The order of operations (BODMAS/PEMDAS) specifies that brackets are evaluated first, then exponents, then multiplication and division, then addition and subtraction.
The sum of the interior angles of any triangle is always 180 degrees.
The quadratic formula x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / 2a solves any quadratic equation of the form ax² + bx + c = 0.
To add fractions with unlike denominators, first find the lowest common denominator and adjust each numerator accordingly.
In a right-angled triangle: sine = opposite/hypotenuse, cosine = adjacent/hypotenuse, and tangent = opposite/adjacent.
The derivative of a function represents the instantaneous rate of change of that function at any given point.
The mean of a data set is the sum of all values divided by the number of values; it is sensitive to extreme outliers.
The probability of two independent events both occurring equals the product of their individual probabilities.
Integration is the inverse of differentiation and is used to calculate areas under curves and total accumulated quantities.
The area of a circle is πr² and its circumference is 2πr, where r is the radius.
The union of sets A and B (A ∪ B) contains all elements in A or B or both; the intersection (A ∩ B) contains only shared elements.
Multiplying an m×n matrix by an n×p matrix yields an m×p result matrix; matrix multiplication is generally not commutative.
A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 with exactly two distinct factors: 1 and itself.
The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right-angled triangle, c² = a² + b², where c is the hypotenuse.
Modular arithmetic considers integers cyclically; a ≡ b (mod n) means a and b leave the same remainder when divided by n.
To convert a percentage to a decimal, divide by 100; to convert a decimal to a percentage, multiply by 100.
In an arithmetic sequence each term differs by a constant amount; in a geometric sequence each term is multiplied by a constant ratio.
The logarithm logₐ(b) is the power to which a must be raised to produce b; natural log (ln) uses base e ≈ 2.718.
A ratio compares two quantities of the same unit; equivalent ratios represent the same proportional relationship scaled differently.
A function maps each element of its domain to exactly one element of its range and is commonly expressed as f(x).
The binomial theorem gives the expanded form of (a + b)ⁿ using coefficients from Pascal's triangle: C(n,k) = n! / (k!(n−k)!).
Complementary angles sum to 90° and supplementary angles sum to 180°.
When multiplying or dividing both sides of an inequality by a negative number, the direction of the inequality sign must be reversed.
The dot product of two vectors equals the product of their magnitudes and the cosine of the angle between them.
Recurring decimals can be expressed as exact fractions using algebraic methods that eliminate the repeating part.
Permutations count ordered arrangements (nPr = n!/(n−r)!); combinations count unordered selections (nCr = n!/(r!(n−r)!)).
The chain rule states that the derivative of a composite function f(g(x)) equals f'(g(x)) multiplied by g'(x).
The sum of interior angles of a polygon with n sides equals (n − 2) × 180 degrees.
The graph of y = ax² + bx + c is a parabola; it opens upward when a > 0 and downward when a < 0.
A complex number has the form a + bi where i = √(−1); complex conjugate pairs share the same real part but have opposite imaginary parts.
Standard deviation measures the spread of data around the mean; a larger value indicates greater variability in the data set.
The Euclidean algorithm finds the greatest common divisor of two integers by repeated application of division and taking the remainder.
An improper fraction has a numerator greater than its denominator and can be converted to a mixed number showing whole parts and a remainder.
Simultaneous equations can be solved by substitution (expressing one variable in terms of another) or by elimination (adding or subtracting equations).
The fundamental theorem of calculus establishes that differentiation and integration are inverse operations, directly linking the two branches of calculus.
Antibiotics treat bacterial infections by killing bacteria or inhibiting their growth; they are ineffective against viral infections.
Beta-blockers reduce heart rate and blood pressure by blocking adrenaline's action on beta-adrenergic receptors in the heart.
A clinical sign is an objective finding observed by a clinician; a symptom is a subjective experience reported by the patient.
Distributive shock, seen in sepsis, results from massive vasodilation and relative hypovolemia despite normal or elevated cardiac output.
Active immunity develops when the body produces its own antibodies after antigen exposure from infection or vaccination.
Nosocomial infections are acquired in a healthcare setting and were not present at the time of the patient's admission.
First-pass metabolism occurs when orally administered drugs are partially broken down by the liver before reaching systemic circulation.
Myocardial infarction occurs when blood supply to part of the heart muscle is blocked, causing ischaemic cell death.
Vaccines stimulate the immune system to produce memory cells so that a faster, stronger response occurs upon future antigen exposure.
In type 1 diabetes, autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells eliminates endogenous insulin production entirely.
An action potential is an all-or-nothing electrical signal along a neuron triggered when the membrane potential exceeds the threshold.
Intravenous drug administration achieves 100% bioavailability because the drug enters systemic circulation directly without first-pass metabolism.
Haemoglobin in red blood cells binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it in tissues where oxygen tension is low.
A type I hypersensitivity reaction is mediated by IgE antibodies and can cause immediate allergic responses including anaphylaxis.
Differential diagnosis is the systematic process of distinguishing between conditions that share similar clinical presentations.
Type A drug reactions are dose-dependent and predictable; type B reactions are idiosyncratic and independent of dose.
The kidneys regulate blood pH by excreting hydrogen ions and reabsorbing bicarbonate ions in the renal tubules.
Sensitivity measures a test's ability to correctly identify those with a disease; specificity measures correct identification of disease-free individuals.
Herd immunity reduces disease transmission when a sufficient proportion of a population has become immune through infection or vaccination.
A competitive antagonist reversibly blocks a receptor's active site; its effect can be overcome by increasing agonist concentration.
Gram-positive bacteria retain crystal violet dye and appear purple; gram-negative bacteria take the counterstain and appear pink.
An ECG records the heart's electrical activity; the P wave represents atrial depolarisation and the QRS complex represents ventricular depolarisation.
Volume of distribution (Vd) indicates how widely a drug distributes into body tissues; a high Vd suggests extensive tissue binding.
Blood pressure is recorded as systolic over diastolic pressure; a normal adult reading is approximately 120/80 mmHg.
Tidal volume is the volume of air inhaled or exhaled in a single normal breath, approximately 500 mL at rest in adults.
The coagulation cascade has intrinsic and extrinsic pathways that converge at factor X, ultimately producing a fibrin clot.
Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host immune response to infection.
Cortisol is released by the adrenal cortex in response to stress and ACTH; it raises blood glucose and suppresses immune responses.
The TNM staging system classifies cancer by tumour size (T), regional lymph node involvement (N), and presence of distant metastasis (M).
A drug's plasma half-life is the time taken for its concentration to fall by half and determines the appropriate dosing interval.
Cytochrome P450 enzymes metabolise many drugs; enzyme inducers accelerate clearance while inhibitors raise plasma drug concentrations.
The oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve shows how haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen varies with the partial pressure of oxygen in tissues.
Antimicrobial resistance develops when antibiotic use selectively favours survival of bacteria that carry resistance genes.
Heart failure occurs when the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's demands and is classified as systolic or diastolic.
Modifiable cardiovascular risk factors include hypertension, smoking, dyslipidaemia, diabetes, obesity, and physical inactivity.
The heart has four chambers: the right and left atria (upper) and the right and left ventricles (lower).
The diaphragm is the primary muscle of respiration; its contraction flattens and increases thoracic volume, driving inhalation.
The adult human skeleton has 206 bones, providing structural support, protecting organs, and enabling movement.
The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions through its sympathetic (fight-or-flight) and parasympathetic (rest-and-digest) divisions.
The cardiac conduction system initiates impulses at the sinoatrial node, passes through the atrioventricular node, then spreads via the bundle of His and Purkinje fibres.
The small intestine is the primary site of nutrient absorption; villi and microvilli greatly increase its absorptive surface area.
Each kidney contains about one million nephrons that filter blood and produce urine through filtration, reabsorption, and secretion.
Lower motor neurons carry signals from the spinal cord to skeletal muscle; their damage causes flaccid paralysis and muscle wasting.
The trachea divides at the carina into left and right bronchi, which branch into bronchioles and terminate in alveoli.
The spleen filters blood, removes aged red blood cells, and stores immune cells including lymphocytes and macrophages.
The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movement and balance by comparing motor commands with sensory feedback and adjusting output.
Synovial joints, such as the knee and shoulder, are freely moveable and have a fluid-filled cavity that lubricates movement.
Arteries carry blood away from the heart at high pressure; veins return blood to the heart and contain one-way valves to prevent backflow.
The hypothalamus controls the pituitary gland via releasing and inhibiting hormones, linking the nervous and endocrine systems.
The skin is the body's largest organ, providing a barrier against pathogens, UV radiation, dehydration, and physical trauma.
Sperm are produced in the seminiferous tubules of the testes and mature as they travel through the epididymis.
At a chemical synapse, neurotransmitters released from the presynaptic terminal diffuse across the cleft and bind postsynaptic receptors.
The liver produces bile for fat emulsification, metabolises nutrients, detoxifies substances, and synthesises plasma proteins.
Skeletal muscle contraction occurs when calcium ions bind troponin, shifting tropomyosin to expose actin binding sites for myosin cross-bridges.
Pulse pressure is the difference between systolic and diastolic blood pressure and reflects stroke volume and arterial compliance.
The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord; the peripheral nervous system connects the CNS to the body.
The islets of Langerhans in the pancreas contain alpha cells (glucagon) and beta cells (insulin), regulating blood glucose levels.
The loop of Henle creates a medullary concentration gradient via countercurrent multiplication, enabling the kidney to produce concentrated urine.
The thymus is a lymphatic organ where T-lymphocytes mature; it is most active during childhood and involutes in adulthood.
Tendons are dense connective tissue that attach skeletal muscle to bone; ligaments connect bone to bone at joints.
A dermatome is the skin region supplied by sensory fibres from a single spinal nerve root; dermatomal maps aid neurological examination.
The ABO blood group system classifies blood into types A, B, AB, and O based on the antigens present on red blood cell surfaces.
The menstrual cycle averages 28 days; ovulation typically occurs around day 14, triggered by a surge in luteinising hormone (LH).
The limbic system, including the hippocampus and amygdala, is involved in memory formation, emotional processing, and motivation.
The stomach secretes hydrochloric acid and pepsinogen to initiate protein digestion and to destroy most ingested microorganisms.
The coronary arteries supply oxygenated blood to the heart muscle; the left and right coronary arteries arise from the root of the aorta.
Anatomical planes include the sagittal (left/right), coronal (front/back), and transverse (upper/lower) planes used to describe body orientation.
The dorsal column-medial lemniscal pathway carries fine touch and proprioception; the spinothalamic tract carries pain and temperature signals.
The large intestine absorbs water and electrolytes from indigestible matter and compacts the remaining material into faeces.
Fibrous joints (such as skull sutures) allow no movement; cartilaginous joints allow slight movement; synovial joints are freely moveable.
Rocks are classified as igneous (cooled magma), sedimentary (compressed sediments), or metamorphic (altered by heat and pressure).
Plate tectonics explains that Earth's lithosphere is divided into moving plates whose interactions produce earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain ranges.
Earth's atmosphere has five main layers: troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere, and exosphere, each with distinct properties.
The greenhouse effect occurs when atmospheric gases absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, raising Earth's surface temperature.
The carbon cycle moves carbon through the atmosphere, oceans, soils, and organisms via photosynthesis, respiration, and geological processes.
The water cycle involves continuous movement of water through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, infiltration, and surface runoff.
Earthquakes release P-waves (compressional) and S-waves (shear); P-waves travel through both solids and liquids, while S-waves travel only through solids.
The Mohs scale ranks mineral hardness from 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond), based on the ability of one mineral to scratch another.
Weathering breaks rocks down through physical, chemical, and biological processes at or near Earth's surface without transporting the material.
Thermohaline circulation drives deep ocean currents by differences in water temperature and salinity that create density gradients.
Fossils form when the hard remains or traces of organisms are buried and preserved within sedimentary rock over geological time.
Volcanic eruptions occur when magma rises through crustal vents, releasing lava, volcanic ash, and gases at the surface.
The principle of superposition states that in undisturbed sedimentary rock sequences, younger layers are always found above older ones.
Soil consists of mineral particles, organic matter (humus), water, air, and a vast community of microorganisms essential for plant growth.
Glaciers form from compacted and recrystallised snow and sculpt landscapes through erosion, sediment transport, and deposition.
Radiometric dating uses the known decay rates of radioactive isotopes to determine the age of rocks and fossils with high precision.
The stratospheric ozone layer absorbs most of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation, protecting surface life from damage.
Rivers erode channels through hydraulic action, abrasion, and solution, transporting and depositing sediment in floodplains and deltas.
Convection currents in Earth's asthenosphere, driven by radioactive heat, are the underlying mechanism that drives tectonic plate movement.
The Richter scale measures earthquake magnitude logarithmically; each whole-number step represents approximately 32 times more energy released.
The rock cycle describes how igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks are continuously transformed into one another over geological time.
Earth's atmosphere is approximately 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, 0.9% argon, and small amounts of carbon dioxide and other gases.
Isostasy describes the gravitational equilibrium between Earth's crust and underlying mantle; thicker crust stands higher, like a floating iceberg.
Biomes are large ecological zones characterised by climate and dominant vegetation type, including rainforest, desert, tundra, and grassland.
Earth's seasons result from its 23.5° axial tilt relative to its orbital plane, not from its varying distance from the Sun.
The eight planets of the solar system, in order from the Sun, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.
Stars form when clouds of gas and dust (nebulae) collapse under gravity, heating the core until nuclear fusion begins.
The big bang model holds that the universe began about 13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense state and has expanded ever since.
The Moon's gravitational pull is the primary driver of Earth's ocean tides; the Sun also contributes to tidal effects.
Stars are classified by spectral type (O, B, A, F, G, K, M) in decreasing order of surface temperature; the Sun is a G-type yellow dwarf star.
A black hole is a region where gravity is so intense that not even light can escape beyond its event horizon.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system, with a mass greater than all other planets combined.
The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy containing hundreds of billions of stars; the Sun is about 26,000 light-years from the galactic centre.
Dark matter does not emit or absorb light but is inferred from its gravitational effects on visible matter and large-scale cosmic structure.
A light-year is the distance light travels in one year through a vacuum, approximately 9.46 × 10¹² km.
A supernova marks the explosive death of a massive star and disperses heavy elements into space, seeding future star and planet formation.
The Hertzsprung-Russell diagram plots stellar luminosity versus surface temperature, revealing life stages from main sequence to giant and white dwarf.
A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon passes between Earth and the Sun; a lunar eclipse occurs when Earth's shadow falls on the Moon.
Kepler's first law states that planets orbit the Sun in ellipses with the Sun at one focus, not in perfect circles.
Cosmological redshift occurs because the universe's expansion stretches the wavelength of light travelling from distant galaxies toward Earth.
The Moon's phases (new, crescent, quarter, gibbous, full) result from the changing angles between the Earth, Moon, and Sun.
The Sun's corona is its outermost atmospheric layer, reaching temperatures exceeding 1 million °C, far hotter than the visible solar surface.
Stellar parallax measures a nearby star's apparent shift against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun, used to calculate stellar distances.
A meteoroid that enters Earth's atmosphere and burns up is a meteor; if it reaches the surface, it is called a meteorite.
The transit method detects exoplanets by measuring the slight dimming of a star's light as a planet passes in front of it from our perspective.
Dark energy is a hypothetical form of energy driving the accelerating expansion of the universe; it may account for about 68% of total energy content.
Comets are icy bodies from the outer solar system that develop bright tails of gas and dust when they travel close to the Sun.
Neutron stars are incredibly dense stellar remnants; a teaspoon of neutron star material would contain a mass of billions of tonnes.
The habitable zone around a star is the orbital range where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface, considered key for life.
The asteroid belt lies between Mars and Jupiter and contains millions of rocky and metallic bodies of varying sizes.
The binary number system uses only 0 and 1 to represent all data processed by digital computers; each digit is called a bit.
The Internet Protocol (IP) assigns unique addresses to devices on a network, enabling data packets to be routed between them globally.
An algorithm is a finite, ordered set of instructions designed to solve a problem or perform a defined computational task.
Encryption transforms readable plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using a key; the correct key is required to decrypt the data.
In supervised machine learning, a model trains on labelled input-output pairs, minimising prediction error to learn a mapping function.
RAM (random access memory) is volatile storage used for active processes; its contents are lost when the computer is powered off.
SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to create, query, update, and delete data in relational database management systems.
HTTP defines the rules for transmitting hypertext data on the web; HTTPS adds TLS encryption to protect the connection.
Object-oriented programming organises software into objects that encapsulate data and behaviour, promoting modularity and code reuse.
Big O notation describes the worst-case growth rate of an algorithm's time or memory usage relative to input size.
The CPU fetches, decodes, and executes program instructions; its speed is measured in gigahertz (GHz) and drives overall system performance.
TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) ensures reliable, ordered data delivery over a network using acknowledgements and retransmission.
Public-key cryptography uses a mathematically linked key pair: data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the private key.
A bit is the smallest unit of digital information (0 or 1); eight bits form one byte, which can represent 256 distinct values.
An artificial neural network consists of interconnected layers of nodes that learn patterns in data by iteratively adjusting connection weights.
Deadlock occurs when two or more processes each hold a resource and wait indefinitely for a resource held by another, halting progress.
Debugging is the process of identifying and fixing errors (bugs) in code; systematic testing and error messages help locate faults.
Cloud computing delivers computing resources over the internet in three main models: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS.
A hash table achieves O(1) average-case time for insertion and lookup by mapping keys to array indices via a hash function.
Wi-Fi uses radio waves to transmit data wirelessly between devices and a router, following the IEEE 802.11 family of standards.
Version control systems like Git track changes to code over time, enabling collaboration, parallel development, and rollback to previous states.
An operating system manages hardware resources and provides services for applications; examples include Windows, macOS, and Linux.
A blockchain is a distributed ledger recording transactions in linked, cryptographically secured blocks, making it resistant to tampering.
An API (Application Programming Interface) defines rules for how software components communicate, enabling integration between different services.
Screen resolution describes the number of pixels displayed horizontally and vertically; higher resolution produces a sharper, more detailed image.
A noun names a person, place, thing, or idea; a verb expresses an action, state, or occurrence.
Etymology is the study of the origins and historical development of words and how their forms and meanings have changed over time.
A semicolon joins two related independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction, providing a stronger pause than a comma.
A metaphor directly identifies one thing with another to highlight a shared quality; a simile makes the comparison using 'like' or 'as'.
Syntax is the set of rules governing the structure and ordering of words and phrases to form grammatically correct sentences.
Active reading involves questioning, annotating, and summarising text, improving comprehension and retention compared to passive reading.
A thesis statement presents the central argument of an essay and guides the content and direction of all supporting paragraphs.
Morphology studies the internal structure of words; morphemes are the smallest meaningful units, including roots, prefixes, and suffixes.
Many English academic words derive from Latin and Greek roots; recognising these roots aids comprehension of unfamiliar technical vocabulary.
Passive voice places the object of an action as the grammatical subject ('The report was written'), reducing clarity when overused.
The present perfect tense (have/has + past participle) connects a past action to the present, as in 'She has completed the module.'
Pragmatics studies how context, tone, and social conventions shape the interpretation of meaning beyond the literal content of words.
Skimming involves reading rapidly for the overall main idea; scanning involves searching systematically for specific facts or keywords.
A well-structured paragraph contains a topic sentence, supporting evidence or details, and a concluding sentence that links back to the main point.
Context clues — surrounding words, phrases, or sentences — can reveal the meaning of unfamiliar terms without consulting a dictionary.
Phonology studies the sound systems of languages, including how phonemes are organised and how their context affects pronunciation.
Citing sources in academic writing credits original authors, supports your argument, and allows readers to trace and verify information.
A subordinate (dependent) clause cannot stand alone as a sentence; it relies on a main clause to convey complete meaning.
Cohesion in writing is achieved through pronoun reference, lexical chains, and connective adverbs that link ideas across sentences.
Common prefixes such as 'pre-' (before), 'post-' (after), 'anti-' (against), and 'semi-' (half) modify root word meanings predictably.
Register refers to the level of formality in language use; academic, professional, and colloquial registers differ in vocabulary and tone.
Homophones are words that sound alike but differ in meaning and often spelling, such as 'their', 'there', and 'they're'.
Discourse markers (e.g. 'furthermore', 'however', 'consequently') signal logical relationships between ideas and improve textual coherence.
Connotation is the emotional or cultural association a word carries beyond its literal (denotative) definition.
The PEEL structure (Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link) provides a framework for building coherent analytical paragraphs in academic writing.
Gregor Mendel's pea plant experiments in the 1860s established the foundational laws of heredity underpinning modern genetics.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 by observing that Penicillium mould inhibited bacterial growth on a contaminated culture plate.
Charles Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859, presenting evidence for evolution through natural selection.
Edward Jenner developed the first successful vaccine in 1796 by demonstrating that cowpox infection conferred protection against smallpox.
The first controlled nuclear chain reaction was achieved at the University of Chicago in December 1942, led by Enrico Fermi.
Nicolaus Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model in 1543, placing the Sun rather than Earth at the centre of the solar system.
Louis Pasteur's germ theory, established in the 1860s, demonstrated that specific microorganisms cause specific infectious diseases.
Marie Curie was the first person to win two Nobel Prizes, earning them in Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911) for her work on radioactivity.
The double-helix structure of DNA was proposed by Watson and Crick in 1953, building on X-ray diffraction data produced by Rosalind Franklin.
Joseph Lister introduced antiseptic surgical technique in the 1860s using carbolic acid, dramatically reducing post-surgical infection rates.
Isaac Newton formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation in his 1687 work 'Principia Mathematica', transforming classical physics.
Dmitri Mendeleev published the first widely accepted periodic table in 1869, correctly predicting properties of then-unknown elements.
General anaesthesia using inhaled ether was first publicly demonstrated in 1846 at Massachusetts General Hospital, making painless surgery possible.
Michael Faraday demonstrated electromagnetic induction in 1831, establishing the principle that underlies electric generators and transformers.
On 12 April 1961, Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space, completing one orbit of Earth aboard the Vostok 1 spacecraft.
Karl Landsteiner discovered the ABO blood group system in 1901, making safe blood transfusions possible by identifying compatibility.
Cell theory, formulated in the 1830s–1850s by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow, states that all living organisms are composed of cells.
Albert Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1905, fundamentally changing the understanding of space, time, mass, and energy.
Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895; within months they were being used medically to image bones without surgery.
Carl Linnaeus developed binomial nomenclature in the 18th century, giving each species a two-part Latin name used universally in biology.
Galen of Pergamon, a 2nd-century Greek physician, dominated anatomical and physiological thinking for over 1,000 years through his extensive writings.
Johannes Gutenberg's movable type printing press, developed around 1440, rapidly spread scientific and medical knowledge across Europe.
The 16th–17th century Scientific Revolution replaced ancient authority with empirical observation and mathematical description of natural phenomena.
Insulin was first isolated by Banting and Best in 1921, transforming type 1 diabetes from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable condition.
Antonie van Leeuwenhoek's microscope improvements in the 1670s enabled the first observations of bacteria, protozoa, and red blood cells.
Spaced repetition schedules review of material at increasing intervals, exploiting the spacing effect to maximise long-term retention.
Focused study sessions of 25–50 minutes followed by short breaks maintain concentration and prevent the buildup of mental fatigue.
Elaborative interrogation improves learning by prompting students to explain why and how facts are true, deepening cognitive processing.
Active recall — retrieving information from memory instead of re-reading — is one of the most effective strategies for long-term retention.
The Cornell method divides notes into a main area, a cue column, and a summary box, encouraging active review and self-testing during revision.
Metacognition — monitoring one's own understanding and adjusting strategies accordingly — is a key predictor of academic achievement.
Sleep consolidates memory by transferring information from short-term to long-term storage; inadequate sleep before exams impairs recall.
Backward planning from the exam date ensures all content is covered; revision blocks are scheduled in reverse from the final deadline.
Minimising digital distractions during study — silencing notifications and using focus apps — significantly increases the quality of learning.
Mnemonics use acronyms, chunking, imagery, or rhymes to link new information to existing memories, aiding recall under exam pressure.
Interleaved practice — alternating between different topics within a session — produces stronger long-term retention than blocked single-topic practice.
Regular aerobic exercise enhances cognitive function, memory consolidation, and stress management, all of which support effective studying.
Practising past exam papers under timed conditions improves time management, reduces test anxiety, and highlights knowledge gaps.
Studying in a consistent, quiet, well-lit space at regular times helps the brain associate that environment with focused, productive learning.
The testing effect shows that retrieving information from memory strengthens neural encoding far more than passively re-reading the same material.
Desirable difficulties — strategies that slow initial learning, such as interleaving and self-testing — produce significantly better long-term retention.
Balanced meals and adequate hydration maintain stable blood glucose, sustaining concentration and cognitive performance throughout a study session.
Dual coding combines verbal explanations with visuals such as diagrams and charts, creating stronger and more accessible memories.
A growth mindset — the belief that abilities improve with effort — correlates with greater academic persistence and resilience when facing challenges.
Writing concise summaries in your own words after studying a topic forces active processing and reveals gaps in understanding.
Concept maps visually organise and connect ideas using nodes and labelled links, helping learners identify relationships between related topics.
Setting specific, measurable study goals (e.g. 'complete 20 practice questions in 30 minutes') improves focus and provides a clear progress measure.
Far transfer — applying knowledge to new, dissimilar problems — requires deep conceptual understanding rather than surface-level memorisation of facts.
Explaining a concept to a peer deepens your own understanding by forcing clear articulation and revealing gaps in your knowledge.
Reviewing notes within 24 hours of a lecture dramatically reduces forgetting by reinforcing memory before significant decay can occur.
